course_description: To enable potential managers to be informed users & providers of management accounting information. Internal accounting terms & procedures; use of accounting data in planning, control & performance evaluation; assumptions & limitations of conventional accounting measurements.

requirements.prerequisites: ACCT7101 or BSAC7101 or COMM7102 or 7301
requirements.incompatible: ACCT3104 or BSAC3104 or 7107 or COMM3402 or 7401 or CO242 or 342 or 814
requirements.assumed_background: Accounting cycle concepts, recording and measuring of accounting transactions.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Lecture Sessions:

Monday 12:00 - 2:50pm; Bldg 32 Room 211 (Gordon Greenwood Bldg)

Thursday 4:00 - 6:50 pm: Bldg 50 Room N201 (Hawken Bldg)

Please Note: Students are required to register for (on si-net) and attend only one of the lecture sessions and one tutorial on a weekly basis.

Students are reminded that lectures are designed to introduce topics. It is your responsibility to make sure you understand the weekly topics by 1) attending the weekly lecture, 2) attending the weekly tutorial, and 3) doing all of the necessary preparatory work and revision work utilising the materials available.

Tutorials will commence in Week 2 . Student's will be advised via email and the course website (under the "announcements" menu item) of any changes to the weekly timetable or other course related issues.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
z

This course has been designed to enable potential managers to make informed decisions in the areas of costing, cost management, planning, control and perfomance measurement. By focusing on basic concepts, analyses, uses and procedures, students will recognise that this course provides many managerial tools for business strategy and implementation. Students will recognise that continued success in any organisation, small or large, requires the use of the managerial accounting concepts and practices learned. They will become informed users and providers of management accounting information which has been developed from a basic knowledge and understanding of relevant management accounting terms and procedures to the use of accounting data in planning, control and performance evaluation activities. We will also consider the assumptions and limitations of conventional accounting methods and measures and consider the behavioural consequences of many different types of performance measurement and control systems.

The ultimate aim of this course is summed up in a quote taken from the Preface of Atkinson, Kaplan, Matsumura and Young's (2007), Management Accounting (5th edition):
" Companies need financial professionals who know how to communicate not only what a company spent but also how it consumed that spending and where it provided value and alignment to strategy. The financial skills that are needed are those which allow us to focus on the future as well as the past, with a common thread of creating value."

learning_outcomes.description: Use managerial accounting information to improve your understanding and decision making capabilities in a variety of costing, cost management, planning and control, and performance measurement situations.
Define and use cost terms, cost classifications and cost behaviour and understand the principles of relevant costing for different types of decisions.
Assign costs to cost objects (e.g., products or services), through the use of outcome/decision appropriate cost tracing and cost allocation mechanisms.

Explain the need for planning and control systems within an organisation, develop master and flexible budgets, and subsequently evaluate organisational and managerial performance using variance analysis and other performance evaluation tools.
Explain the issues surrounding organisaton structure choice and the related issues of transfer pricing and performance measurement that can arise as a result of the decision to decentralise.

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
In addition to the lectures, tutorials and consultation you should make regular use of the following resources to do well in the course:

The problem for self-study with solution that appears at the end of each chapter in the text (Horngren) you are assigned to read. These problems are a useful learning aid.

The companion web site for the assigned text provides a number of student resources.The web site address for Horngren et al (13th ed.) is:
http://www.prenhall.com/horngren/cost13e

Select a chapter of the text you wish to access. The screen will provide you with the objectives of the chapter and a menu of available resources at the site.

The site helps you to review what you have learned in the chapter by posing true/false, fill in the blanks, and multiple choice questions that you can answer and submit for marking. It would be valuable for you to attempt these questions after you have read the chapter but before you have attempted any of the tutorial questions, exercises or problems assigned. There are also essay questions that will assist you in learning the concepts of the chapter. The essays are not graded but provide a model answer after you have submitted your answer. These resources will also be beneficial for your assessment in ACCT7107.

There are also internet exercises that you may wish to access.

The student resources allow you to access the spreadsheet templates for selected problems in the text as well as information on how to use the template to answer the problems.

Note: The instructors resources are for staff only and are password protected.

The MyAccountingLab is an online practice and assessment tool desigend to help students practice cost and managerial accounting problems and concepts and receive feedback on their performance - think of it as your own tutorial experience! The access code to this resource comes with the purchase of your text. The website is: www.myaccountinglab.com More details of this resource will be provided in class and on the course BB website.

The student Study Guide that accompanies the text is also a useful resource.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

The following feedback provided to students enrolled within this course, during the semester :
(i) individual quiz component marks and total quiz marks out of 10% (immediate feedback in your gradebook);

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment
Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment is not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers. Please follow the procedure below:

Students visit the UQ Business School Reception area, and complete a Viewing of Examination form.

The original form is kept by the School Office and a copy is given to the student.

Completed forms must be submitted to the School Office by the end of the first week of the subsequent semester at the latest.

Examination papers of those students who have submitted a completed form to the School Office will be available for viewing during the following relevant session.

The sessions are scheduled as follows: in the case of first semester examinations during the third teaching week in semester two; and in the case of second semester examinations and summer examinations during the third teaching week in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands (see below).

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation. Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The location and the hours during which papers may be viewed will be published on the School's website and will be available from the School Reception area.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results
Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the course co-ordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the course co-ordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Director of Studies of the BEL Faculty.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

COMPUTING FACILITIES
Courses offered by the School of Business extensively utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich for further details.

Other Course Guidelines

In all examinations within the course, students will NOT be allowed to use alpha-numeric programmable calculators.

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Before attempting this course, students are advised that it is important to complete the appropriate prerequisite course(s) listed on the front of this course profile. No responsibility will be accepted by the School of Business, the Faculty of Business, Economics and Law or the University of Queensland for poor student performance occurring in courses where the appropriate prerequisite(s) has/have not been completed, for any reason whatsoever.

It is the student's responsibility to attend classes and consult designated noticeboards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including examination times. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

The course will be (loosely) divided into two sections: cost measurement and management systems, and planning and control systems. The objective of the cost measurement and management systems is to understand how to develop cost information to assist in a variety of managerial decisions. We will consider cost definitions and behaviour and how decision making requires consideration of "relevant" costs. We will consider the differences between the "cost" information requirements for financial accounting and external reporting purposes, and the "cost" information for managerial accounting. The study of costing systems starts with traditional costing systems still in widespread use today. Potential problems associated with these traditional systems within certain business environments are then examined. Through the use of lecture materials, the assigned text, readings and a case, the group will discuss situations in which the use of traditional costing systems provide seriously flawed cost information resulting in management decisions that were inappropriate and often at a significant detriment to the firm. We will then examine the refinements that can be made to these costing systems, such as activity based costing and acknowledge how the improved information leads to more appropriate managerial decision making. It is all about learning that there are different costs (and systems) to be used for different purposes!

The second section of the course covers the use of accounting information in the planning and control of organisational units and in the assessment of their performance. Planning is considered through the development and use of budgets and flexible budgets. By making comparisons of actual and budgeted performance and through the use of variance analysis, students will be introduced to the concept of control. You will learn to interpret how actual performance has deviated from budget and then ponder the more important question, why? Answers to such questions are of course important feedback to management in the improvement of future performance. We will also consider the impact of organisational structure and the choice of responsibility centres on the analysis and evaluation of both the organisation and its managers. Associated with these choices, we will consider the issues surrounding transfer pricing and sub-unit (and managerial) performance measurement.

Note:
The delivery focus adopted in Managerial Accounting and Control is based on learning rather than teaching. The primary focus of learning is the acquisition of knowledge with concept learning providing the necessary structure for you to acquire and build the relevant knowledge. This focus will be achieved by requiring you to be responsible for your own learning in terms of accessing lecture and course material through various mediums, undertaking to complete the set tutorial exercises and problems within the set timeframe, attending all scheduled sessions, and satisfactorily completing all formal assessment requirements. As many of the weeks' materials require the knowledge acquired from the previous week it is extremely important that you keep up with the weekly material and seek help if you are falling behind. The weekly tutorial questions should be used to guide your study for the week and should be prepared each week in advance of your tutorial to gain the maxiumum advantage. Student's will also have the benefit of accessing the assigned text's myaccountinglab website. This website allows students to access a variety of learning materials, questions and quizzes that relate to each of the text chapters covered in the course. Access to the site is gained from a code purchased with the assigned course text (or purchased separately through the my accounting lab website). The benefit of this resource is that student's (in their own time) can study a particular topic, test their knowledge through the questions and quizzes provided and be given immediate feedback as to their stronger and weaker areas of performance, thus allowing them to concentrate their future study efforts in areas that are going to provide the greatest overall benefit. No course assessment is allocated to completion of tasks using this resource but it is highly recommended and encouraged!

Student's should also ensure that the eLearning (available on your course blackboard website) self-assessment quizzes are attempted by their respective due dates. This will provide important feedback to you on your level of understanding and may further highlight particular areas in which you are experiencing difficulty. This can then be addressed by further self-study or in conjunction with the lecturer. Do not underestimate the benefits of this resource. To highlight the importance of this resource, assessment is allocated to the completion of the online quizzes.

There will be weekly lectures of approximately three hours duration. Copies of the lecture slides (3 to a page) will be provided to you each week at your lecture session. These slides help place a framework around the weekly material and highlight the areas of particular importance. It is recommended of course, that you attend the weekly lectures to ensure you are getting the maximum amount of information about the course and that you are participating in the additional exercises, discussions etc. so that you gain the most from the course.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

other_learning_activities_information:
Tutorial/Small Group Sessions
Tutorials/Small group sessions in this course are conducted each week commencing in Teaching Week 2. Class sizes are limited. Times and registration for these sessions are available through mySI-Net. You must sign on for your preferred session electronically. Should you experience problems, please contact the teaching staff in Teaching Week 1. Once you are included in a group, you should attend that group only each week. Changes will be permitted only in exceptional circumstances, and only after discussion with teaching staff.
It is the Course Coordinator's expectation that tutorials in this course will be conducted in a professional and conscientious manner. It is not expected that every question listed in the tutorial column of the learning activities (section 4.1 of the course profile) will be fully discussed by the tutor each week. The solutions to these questions will be placed on the course website each week after the relevant lecture (Thursday session). Student's are expected to use these questions and solutions to assist with their understanding and learning of the weekly concepts, issues and problems. If student's have difficulties with any of these assigned questions (and the suggested solutions) this problem should be raised with the tutor in the tutorial. The tutor can then assist students with the correct interpretation and working of the question(s). If there are no issues with these questions, the tutor will work through the Additional in Tute Exercises found in the "Other" column of the learning activities (section 4.1) as time permits and student participation will be encouraged. Soltuions to these questions will be released on BB after the weeks tutorials have been completed.

In summary, it is expected that Tutorials will run as follows: 1) cover any issues with assigned tutorial questions assigned in the learning activities section 4.1 (as answers to these questions will be placed on the course website after the relevant lecture so that students can do the exercises and problems using the solutions wisely as a reference point) ; and 2) as time permits cover as many of the new "additonal in tute exercises". It will be expected and assumed that students have attempted all of the assigned tutorial questions BEFORE the tute.

Consultation
Teaching staff are there to assist you. Staff members have set consultation times during which you may see them without making an appointment. Consultation times for staff are posted on the course website and on individual staff member's doors. Consultation outside of these times is by appointment only.
If you have questions or points that require clarification, you may visit during consultation times. If staff members are unavailable it is easiest to send them an email.
Where applicable, you may also use the course website online Discussion Board to seek assistance from both your peers and staff. All content related discussions will take place in the Main topic that is a public forum that everyone in the course is able to access and read.

Administration
All content related questions and specific questions concerning the administration of this course should, in the first instance be directed to the tutor/s involved in delivery of the course. Content matters that cannot be dealt with by the tutor should then be referred to the course co-ordinator. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School Office on Level 2 Colin Clark Building, St Lucia Campus.

Students should access the "announcements" on the course website regularly to keep abreast of any important changes or issues relating to the course.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made in writing (not via email) to the Course Coordinator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application together with the Course Coordinator's and the student's signatures and a copy returned to the student.

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% (0.4 of a grade) of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue.

The above late submission information does not apply to honours theses, 8 unit research theses, 4 unit research projects and 2 unit research reports. For extension and late submission information in relation to these courses see section 5.4 of the course profile.
other_assessment_information:
Mid-semester Exams
Mid-semester exams are the responsibility of the Course Coordinator. The Faculty has no jurisdiction over mid-semester examinations, hence all correspondence and documentation needs to be forwarded directly to the Course Coordinator. A student who fails to sit the mid-semester exam at the scheduled time will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the Course Coordinator is supplied with documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence either prior to, or no later than two working days after, the mid-semester examination. In such cases, if the Course Coordinator is satisfied with the documentation AND the student has successfully completed Quiz 1, then the final exam will be re-weighted to the total of both the original mid-semester and original final ie., the final exam will constitute 90% of the students overall grade. There are no written special mid-semester examinations in the UQ Business School.

Final Exams
A student who is unable to sit or complete a scheduled final examination must apply for a special examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held. Applications should be lodged at a Student Centre for submission to the Examinations Section, who will forward it to the appropriate Faculty/School administration. For evidence required and time limits see Assessment Rules 1A.14 as listed in the University of Queensland Handbook. Faculties may approve applications for special examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Special examinations are not automatically granted. All applications are assessed on a case-by-case basis and may require an interview where it is considered necessary to appraise the student's position in more detail. Directors of Studies will identify and provide appropriate advice to students applying repeatedly for special examinations, including referral to Student Support Services and the Disability Program.
